title: South China Sea: If you can’t beat them, build islands
gist: South China Sea claimants have learned China's lesson — Vietnam, Malaysia and Taiwan are accelerating their own island-building because reclamation changes facts on the ground with few consequences, normalizing a competitive militarisation dynamic that raises escalation risk.

insights.claim: Vietnam, Malaysia and Taiwan have each accelerated their own South China Sea land reclamation, signalling a new regional status quo of competitive island building rather than opposition to physical alteration of disputed features.
insights.speaker: Nitya Labh
insights.so_what: Reclamation is no longer a uniquely Chinese behaviour — a broadening, harder-to-condemn regional trend.

insights.claim: China has demonstrated that states can change facts on the ground with limited international consequences, and the greater danger is island-building becoming normalized as an instrument of regional competition.
insights.speaker: Nitya Labh
insights.so_what: Normalization, not scale, is the escalation vector to watch across all claimants.

insights.claim: Vietnam has added about 216 new hectares of land, 15 harbours, ammunition depots and a 2.5-kilometre runway at Barque Canada Reef, making its dredging and reclamation the second largest in the region after China.
insights.speaker: Nitya Labh
insights.so_what: Vietnam is now the region's #2 reclaimer with military-grade infrastructure — a material capability shift.
insights.locator: over the past several years Vietnam has begun

insights.claim: Malaysia has built a 1,368-metre airstrip at Swallow Reef and claims at least 12 Spratly features but has a physical presence on only five.
insights.speaker: Nitya Labh
insights.so_what: Malaysia is quietly militarising even while protesting Vietnam — watch its occupied-feature build-out.

insights.claim: Taiwan conducted a 3-hectare reclamation on Itu Aba supporting a major wharf, a 1,200-metre airstrip, lighthouse and hospital.
insights.speaker: Nitya Labh
insights.so_what: Even Taiwan is expanding physical presence, widening the set of reclaiming actors.
insights.locator: Taiwan has also pursued its own island building

insights.claim: China's South China Sea land reclamation is estimated at more than around 2,210 hectares, remaining without parallel in both scale and its connection to Beijing's expansive claims.
insights.speaker: Nitya Labh
insights.so_what: Anchors the scale gap — other claimants' activity is real but an order of magnitude smaller.

insights.claim: In 2024 Malaysia complained to Vietnam's foreign ministry about Hanoi's Spratly expansions while continuing its own reclamation, showing states increasingly distinguish rivals' reclamation from legitimising their own.
insights.speaker: Nitya Labh
insights.so_what: The double standard illustrates how quickly reclamation norms are eroding among claimants themselves.

insights.claim: Weak enforcement of the 2016 arbitral ruling reinforced the perception among claimants that international law alone cannot secure disputed territory, lowering the political cost of reclamation.
insights.speaker: Nitya Labh
insights.so_what: The unenforced ruling paradoxically incentivized more facts-on-the-ground land grabs.
insights.locator: Take, for example, the 2016 arbitral tribunal

insights.claim: If claimants believe a South China Sea Code of Conduct is near completion, current island building reflects a 'race to the finish line' to maximise claims before an agreement locks positions.
insights.speaker: Nitya Labh
insights.so_what: CoC progress could accelerate, not calm, reclamation in the near term — a counterintuitive risk.
insights.locator: For those who believe the Code of Conduct is nearing completion

insights.claim: Absent a formalised Code of Conduct, competitive island building resembles a classic security dilemma that produces cumulative militarisation and raises the threat of war even if no participant sought confrontation.
insights.speaker: Nitya Labh
insights.so_what: A self-reinforcing militarisation loop raises tail risk of a South China Sea flashpoint.
